text: C-84 - Reactors-Special Features of Aircraft Reactors
C-84 - Reactors-Special Features of Aircraft Reactors
The Aircraft Reactor Experiment (ARE) was successfully concluded in November of 1954, and a detailed report of the operation was published the following year.
1, 2, and 3 for reference use in visualizing the disassembly process.
The nuclear operation of the ARE was concluded at 8:04 PM, Friday, November 12, 1954, upon insertion of the scram rods.

While it was subsequently shown that the diaphragm of the main fuel pump pressure transmitter was ruptured, it is believed that this occurred during the early morning of November 13 and that prior to that time the activity had been leaking from one or more of the potential leaks in or around the main fuel pump (spark plugs, seals, fill lines, vent lines, etc.).

In order to keep these gases from leaking from the cell into occupied areas of the building (the cell proved to be quite porous), it was necessary to maintain the cell at a subatmospheric pressure (-2 to -4 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ ) by the use of a jet air pump which pumped around 100 cfm of air from the cell.

This air, with its activity, was discharged some 1000 ft south of the ARE building, but the negligible wind velocity that existed at times during the period after shutdown was not sufficient to cause adequate dispersion of this activity.

In order to determine whether the fuel in all six parallel reactor passages was removed, the flush carrier was heated to a temperature which was $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ hotter than the fuel system so that the passage of the carrier through each of the six parallel reactor tubes could be observed by thermocouples on these tubes.
4) did not clear, the multipoint temperature record of all tubes indicated that the carrier had passed through all tubes.
4 was subsequently found to be inoperative.
After the fuel was dumped, the increased level of the airborne activity caused all but a few of the operating personnel who were wearing gas masks to evacuate the building for about 1 hr.

section: DISASSEMBLY OF THE REACTOR AND AUXILIARY SYSTEMS
text: The postoperative examination of the ARE system started on December 10, 1954, with the taking of a fuel sample from the dump tank while the fuel was still molten.

In order to separate the fuel circuit from the sodium circuit for the purpose of dismantling the sodium system, two flat lead shields, 6 ft high, 4 ft wide, and 2 in. thick, were suspended on beams which ran the width of the heat exchanger cell.
For work in the higher radiation fields associated with the fuel system, a lead box was built that was 2 in. thick on four sides and the bottom.
One end of the bottom had a 6-in.-wide slot, and one side had an 8-in.-square slot, 9 in. off the bottom, through which personnel worked.
2, was started on January 18, 1955, at which time the radiation level was down to 30 to $250\mathrm{mr/hr}$ with the lead shields hanging between the sodium system and the fuel system.
The main sodium pump was removed first, and it was found that the rotary element had a radiation reading of 12 mr/hr at contact with the impeller.
After the rotary assembly had been cleaned in a bath of $50\%$ kerosene and $50\%$ methyl alcohol, the impeller was removed and submitted for examination.
The standby sodium pump was removed as a unit with 6-in. stubs left on all lines so that the pump could be salvaged for use in other experiments.
At the time it was removed, this pump, which had not operated during nuclear operation of the reactor, had a radiation reading of $1\mathrm{mr/hr}$ at contact with the bowl and the top flange.
The ends of the cut sodium lines read 2 mr/hr at contact.

After the removal of the sodium system pumps and heat exchangers, it was found that the radiation field had increased to about $600\mathrm{mr / hr}$ The equipment had helped to shield the area from the fuel system radiation.
Sodium lines 304 through 309, 313, and 314 were then removed in as long lengths as possible and sealed at the cut ends from air and moisture.
Radiation levels on these lines were 2 to $10\mathrm{mr / hr}$ .
During the removal of these sodium lines the flat lead shields were adequate to protect personnel from the radiation from the fuel system, as shown in Fig.5.
Sodium lines 301 and 302 were removed by personnel working within the lead box.

The radiation level at the open ends of lines 301 and 302, which were cut where they entered the tank pit wall, was only $2\mathrm{mr/hr}$ , but the radiation field in that region outside the lead box was 700 to $1000\mathrm{mr/hr}$ .
Sodium lines 303 and 310, the lines to and from the reactor, were removed next.
Because of the position of line 303, it was necessary to sever it where it entered the reactor cell wall by using an electric arc.
A small amount of air activity, 2 divisions on the 2K scale of the air monitor, was observed as a consequence of the sodium fire.
Both line 303 and line 310, which was cut with a hack saw by a craftsman working from the lead box, showed radiation levels of $2\mathrm{mr/hr}$ at contact with the ends of the lines.
The radiation level of the main sodium-pump lubricating system was $2\mathrm{mr / hr}$ but this was found to be surface contamination.

A complete radiation survey of the fuel system, made on February 14, 1955, showed $75\mathrm{r / hr}$ at contact with the insulation on the rotameter of the main fuel pump, $55\mathrm{r / hr}$ at contact with insulation on the dead leg on the bottom of the pump bowl, and $12\mathrm{r / hr}$ at contact with the top flange.
1, and a photograph of the cell containing the fuel pumps is presented in Fig.
Removal of the rotary assembly caused a small burst of air activity that cleared in 2 to 3 min.
The radiation level of the rotary assembly was $20\mathrm{r / hr}$ at 5 ft.
The radiation level of the main fuel pump bowl was $900\mathrm{mr / hr}$ at 5 ft.
operation at power, and therefore the radiation level on the bottom of the pump bowl was only $16\mathrm{mr / hr}$ .
The radiation levels of both exchangers were $10\mathrm{r / hr}$ at 6 in.

The open ends of these fuel lines had radiation readings of $10\mathrm{r / hr}$ at 3 in.

Lines in the standby fuel circuit were free of fuel and showed only surface contamination of $2\mathrm{mr / hr}$ The reactor supply and discharge lines were cut with hack saws by craftsmen working within the lead box.

On March 10, 1955, the concrete pit plugs were removed from the top of the reactor pit and the concrete blocks which lined the walls and bottom of the reactor cell were lifted out to provide space for the lead box.
The helium manifold and the shim and regulating rod chambers had radiation readings of $200\mathrm{mr / hr}$ at contact.
These lines had radiation readings of 10 r/hr at 3 in. when removed.
The sodium lines had radiation readings of $300\mathrm{mr/hr}$ at contact.

The exposed reactor pressure shell showed 6 r/hr at 6 in.

With this equipment, a period of five 8-hr days was required to cut the 2-in.-thick pressure shell.

section: DISMANTLING OF THE FILL AND DRAIN TANKS
text: (One of the fuel-carrier tanks was not used.) A radiation survey showed 18 r/hr at contact with the top of the tank,

section: DISMANTLING OF THE FILL AND DRAIN TANKS
text: 990 r/hr in one of the cooling tubes through the tank, and 84 r/hr at contact with insulation on the side of the tank.

section: DISMANTLING OF THE FILL AND DRAIN TANKS
text: After the dump tank and fuel lines had been removed, the radiation level in the pit was 20 to $50\mathrm{mr/hr}$ .

section: DISMANTLING OF THE FILL AND DRAIN TANKS
text: One of the fuel carrier tanks, however, had a radiation level of $100\mathrm{mr/hr}$ and was therefore sent to storage.
Most of the equipment cleaned in this manner was later disposed of in the burial ground because of radiation levels of about $2\mathrm{mr / hr}$
Since the tank was at a temperature of between 1200 and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the radiation level was high, the drilling had to be done from atop the cell plugs.
To remove the fuel sample, a 3/8-in.-dia Inconel tube was passed through the opening in the tank, and a small vacuum pump was used to pull fuel into the tube.
14, which had surrounded a fuel tube in the central region of the reactor, has many visible cracks and one half of the block had fractured.

When final disposition of the reactor was to be made in October 1957, it was found that, as a result of atmospheric action on the adsorbed sodium, the blocks had deteriorated to the point where they had lost their structural strength.

The main and standby fuel pump pressure transmitters (PXT-6 and PXT-7) were examined for calibration shifts, zero shift, radiation damage, and mechanical damage.

section: ACTIVATION ANALYSES OF SODIUM SYSTEM SAMPLES
text: Two sections of pipe containing residual sodium and a sample of sodium were examined for gamma activity after a decay time of 206 days, by using a sodium iodide gamma-ray spectrometer.

section: ACTIVATION ANALYSES OF SODIUM SYSTEM SAMPLES
text: The data obtained are presented in Table 2.
Bell et al.,[12] the presence of $\mathrm{Xe}^{135}$ , $\mathrm{Xe}^{138}$ , and $\mathrm{Kr}^{88}$ was revealed by their own or their descendants' gamma radiation.
The only activity clearly identified was due to Ru<sup>103</sup>, which is characterized by a 0.50-Mev gamma ray and a 40-day half life.
A similar study was made of three small samples cut from the reactor fuel inlet line (line 120).
Gamma spectrometry showed $\mathsf{Ru}^{103}$ , $\mathsf{Ru}^{106}$ , and $\mathsf{Zr}^{95}$ - $\mathsf{Nb}^{95}$ as the only identifiable activities.

Since a value of 6 or 7 is usually assumed for $n$ , it is apparent that the measurements of the specific activity of the fuel are essentially in agreement with the values obtained on small samples with a "cutie pie."

In order to estimate the efficiency of retention of typical fission products, the radiochemical analyses reported on ARE fuel were compared with similar results obtained on a sample of solid fluoride fuel $(\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}, 8.5\mathrm{wt}\% \mathrm{U})$ irradiated in hole 12 of the ORNL Graphite Reactor.
The following ratios were then obtained between analyses reported for the ARE fuel and those reported for the standard sample (designated as MR-1):

Apparently $\mathsf{Sr}^{89}$ , a descendant of 2.6-min $\mathsf{Kr}^{89}$ , was reduced by a factor of about 2 below the expected level in the ARE, presumably due to partial escape of its noble gas ancestor.
The low value of $\mathsf{Zr}^{95}$ has not been explained.
It is of interest to point out that the amount of $\mathsf{Zr}^{95}$ reported radiochemically was in good agreement with the amount deduced from the decay data on solid fuel.

It must also be mentioned that the amount of $\mathsf{Zr}^{95}-\mathsf{Nb}^{95}$ found on the walls of the fuel circuit was apparently negligible compared with the amount found in the fuel (about 1 part in 2000 if the wall activity was taken to be uniform over the entire surface).
The very low $\mathsf{Ru}^{103}$ value in the ARE sample may possibly have been due to a faulty analysis, but it was felt to be real.

The agreement was fair between radiochemical results from the ARE dump-tank material for $\mathsf{Ce}^{141}$ and $\mathsf{La}^{140}$ and the results deduced from decay of the solid sample of ARE dump-tank material.
since $Xe^{136}$ is stable and has a very low thermal-neutron absorption cross section (0.15 barns).
On the other hand, $Cs^{137}$ is the daughter of 3.9-min $Xe^{137}$ .
Thus, a difference between the ratios of the amounts of these isotopes in the two samples was a measure of the escape from the ARE fuel of $Xe^{137}$ .
The results indicate that not over 20% of the $Xe^{137}$ escaped from the fuel, and that, possibly, none did.
It has become customary18,19 to describe the release of xenon from the fluoride fuels in terms of a quantity $\lambda_{\mathfrak{p}}$ defined by
This value is consistent with the observed behavior of $\mathsf{C}\mathsf{s}^{137}$ also.
From the $\mathsf{Sr}^{89}$ data reported above it appears that krypton isotopes have larger values of $\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}$ than do xenon isotopes.

If all $\mathsf{Ru}^{103}$ is removed from the fuel by the walls, and if the ruthenium "plate" is uniform over the entire reactor, the approximate rate of deposition of $\mathsf{Ru}^{103}$ is 0.7 $(P / A)$ $\mu /\mathrm{hr}$ , where $P$ is the total reactor power in Mw and $A$ is the surface area in cm².
